summary: The Mishna details the most physically demanding Temple act: the HP's incense transfer technique inside the Holy of Holies - fingertips on the ladle, thumbs drawing it in, tipping into bare palms. The Gemara then discusses Passover registration (nimnaim): withdrawal is permitted before slaughter; after slaughter, 'according to the sheep' no longer applies. This parallels the replacement HP question: slaughter changes the legal relationship to the initiated act.

display.whats: How does the HP transfer incense from the ladle into his bare palms inside the Holy of Holies?
display.hint: The Mishna details the most physically demanding Temple act: gripping the ladle with fingertips, tilting it between the arms, and using the thumbs to pour incense into cupped palms.
display.title: Fingertips and Thumbs: The Physical Challenge of the Incense Transfer

learning.ahaMoment: The kometz was called the hardest Temple service because the HP had to hold the ladle with fingertips and pour with thumbs into bare palms - extraordinary dexterity required.
learning.memoryAnchor: HP tilts ladle between forearms, draws handle with thumbs toward body, tips incense into bare cupped palms - the most awkward act in the Temple.
learning.learnerQuestion: How did the High Priest transfer the incense from the ladle into his bare palms without using his thumbs as scoops?
learning.coreTension: The incense must end up cupped in the High Priest's palms, but his thumbs are occupied gripping the spoon and cannot be used for scooping, making the transfer a balance-and-tip maneuver rather than a simple pour.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara describes the High Priest balancing the ladle on his fingertips while using his thumbs to tip it, demonstrating that the transfer requires an extraordinary technique - not an ordinary pour - and is part of why the kometz is called the most difficult service.

takeaway.text: The High Priest transferred incense from ladle to palms by balancing the ladle on his fingertips and tipping it with his thumbs - a technically demanding maneuver that contributes to the kometz being called the most difficult Temple service.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may picture an ordinary pour from the ladle into the hands, missing that the thumb-grip constraint forces a balance-and-tip technique that is the source of the service's extreme difficulty.

argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: HP grips ladle top with fingertips, places it between forearms, draws handle with thumbs, tips incense into palms.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: HP grips ladle top with fingertips, places

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: This explains why the kometz was called the most difficult service - extraordinary manual dexterity with no vessel allowed.
argumentFlow.label: Explanation: This explains why the kometz was called

halachic: incense placed by bare hands not vessel

misconceptions.misconception: The HP could pour the incense into his hands normally, using the ladle like a cup.
misconceptions.correction: The physical difficulty was enormous: the HP had to balance the ladle on his fingertips while using his thumbs to tip it - he could not use a normal grip. This is why the Mishna calls the kometz the hardest Temple service.

quizSeeds.answer: The HP had to grip the ladle with his fingertips, draw the handle with his thumbs, and tip incense into his bare cupped palms - no vessel allowed, requiring extraordinary manual dexterity.
quizSeeds.question: Why was the kometz called the most difficult Temple service?

sugyot.finalRuling: The HP must use bare hands to transfer incense; no vessel is permitted for this step.

display.whats: The Passover registration system and whether withdrawal is valid after slaughter
display.hint: The Gemara discusses who may register on the Passover offering and when withdrawal is permitted. This connects to the replacement HP question: can an act be completed by a different agent than the one who initiated it?
display.title: 'According to the Sheep': Registration Must Happen While It Is Alive

learning.ahaMoment: Passover registration and replacement HP share the structural question: once an act is initiated under one person's authority, can another person complete or join it?
learning.memoryAnchor: Can you register on a slaughtered sheep? The Gemara's no answer illuminates the replacement HP question.
learning.learnerQuestion: Can a person join the registered group of a Passover offering after the sheep has already been slaughtered?
learning.coreTension: The verse says the lamb is counted 'according to its eating,' which could be read as permitting registration at any point before the meal including after slaughter, but the Gemara restricts the timing to while the animal is still alive.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara reads 'according to the sheep' as requiring registration while the animal is still alive - 'according to the sheep' means in reference to the living animal, and once it is slaughtered, the window for joining its group has closed.

takeaway.text: Registration on a Passover offering must take place while the animal is alive - 'according to the sheep' means in reference to the living animal, and post-slaughter registration is invalid.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume registration can happen at any point before the meal including after slaughter, missing that the Gemara derives a pre-slaughter deadline from the phrase 'according to the sheep.'

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Can someone register on the Passover offering after it has been slaughtered?
argumentFlow.label: Question: Can someone register on the Passover offering

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'According to the sheep' means while alive - after slaughter the registration relationship ends.
argumentFlow.label: Principle: 'According to the sheep' means while alive

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Similarly: the replacement HP cannot simply 'join' the first HP's initiated service.
argumentFlow.label: Application: Similarly: the replacement HP cannot simply 'join'

halachic: slaughtered animal cannot be registered on

misconceptions.misconception: A person may join the registered group of a Passover offering at any point before the meal, including after the sheep has already been slaughtered.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara rules that registration must happen while the animal is alive - 'according to the sheep' means in reference to the living animal, so post-slaughter registration is invalid.

visualizableElements.description: Passover registration: withdrawal valid before slaughter, invalid after - the slaughter is the point of no return

quizSeeds.answer: Slaughter ends the possibility of new registration on an offering. Similarly, the replacement HP cannot simply join the first HP's initiated service - the question of a new bull turns on this parallel.
quizSeeds.question: What does the Passover registration discussion reveal about the replacement HP?

sugyot.finalRuling: After slaughter, new registration on a Passover offering is not possible; this parallels the replacement HP question.

rashiTranslations.en: How he performs the second incense scooping inside - from the ladle into his palms: he grips the ladle top with his fingertips.
After placing the coal pan on the ground, he holds the ladle handle between his forearms and lifts with both thumbs.
He draws the handle toward his body with his thumbs until the handle is between his elbows and the ladle head reaches palm level.
He then turns it sideways into his palms and piles the incense onto the coals.
He tilts the ladle sideways into his cupped palms and transfers the incense onto the coal pan.
They register - whoever wishes to register on the Passover offering does so; whoever wishes to withdraw may do so.
Before slaughter: anyone who wants to register may join; anyone who wants to withdraw from the group may do so.
Until it is slaughtered - withdrawal is permitted at any time up to the slaughter.
If it is so - that after slaughter one can still be 'counted on the sheep'...
Registered on the sheep - 'according to the sheep' means while it is alive.
If they want the registered group to diminish they may withdraw...
While its being (hiyyut) is intact - while the animal is alive they may withdraw from registration.
Not after slaughter - once slaughtered, 'according to the sheep' no longer applies.
They do not redeem a firstborn donkey...
Not with a calf nor with a wild animal - because the verse says 'a sheep.'
Not with a slaughtered sheep - for the verse implies a living sheep.
Not with kilayim - a crossbreed of goat and ewe, even though from both sides it is 'sheep.'
Not with a koi (uncertain creature) - since 'male goat and deer' are mentioned but 'not a slaughtered one.'
Apparently a slaughtered animal is not called 'a sheep' - the same logic applies to the HP's bull.
He took them out to the market - folded/removed them from the wall.
This last point concludes the parallel discussion about what 'a bull/sheep' means after slaughter.
